enrichment.short_description: Open-source enterprise wiki and structured-content platform that lets teams document, organize, and build lightweight applications on top of a shared knowledge base.
enrichment.starting_price: Free self-hosted Community edition; paid support and cloud plans start at roughly 2 euros per user per month, billed annually
enrichment.seo_title: XWiki Review: Open-Source Wiki and App Platform, Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what XWiki offers: a free, open-source enterprise wiki for documentation and knowledge management, plus its app-building tools, pricing tiers, and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: XWiki is an open-source enterprise wiki and structured-content platform originally released in January 2004 by Ludovic Dubost, who founded the company behind it, now known as XWiki SAS, headquartered in Paris, France with a development team also based in Iasi, Romania. Unlike simple documentation wikis, XWiki combines page-based collaborative editing with an application-development layer, letting technical and semi-technical users build structured data forms, workflows, and small internal apps directly on top of wiki pages using XWiki's scripting and macro system. Core capabilities include WYSIWYG and wiki-syntax editing, OpenDocument-based import and export, page annotations and tagging, granular rights and permissions management, full version history, and support for running many interlinked wikis as a wiki farm. XWiki is licensed under LGPL and can be self-hosted for free with no user limits, which makes it popular with government agencies, universities, and privacy-conscious organizations that want to avoid vendor lock-in on their knowledge base. XWiki SAS, the commercial entity behind the project, sells paid support subscriptions and managed cloud hosting on top of the free core. These commercial tiers are Starter (cloud only, up to 25 users), Basic (cloud or on-premise, up to 1,000 users), Business (cloud or on-premise, up to 10,000 users, includes OIDC and SAML single sign-on and Confluence or SharePoint migration tooling), and Enterprise (unlimited users, advanced deployment options including Kubernetes, dedicated account management). All paid tiers add early security patches, premium extensions, and an antivirus application not available in the free Community edition. XWiki also offers consulting and training services billed per day, and provides a 50 percent discount on support along with free Starter-tier cloud hosting for qualifying open-source projects and NGOs. The platform is extensible through a large marketplace of community-built extensions and applications, and exposes a RESTful API for integrating with external systems. Its main audience is IT departments, knowledge management teams, and software organizations that need an enterprise-grade, self-hostable alternative to Confluence, SharePoint, or MediaWiki, especially where data residency, customization depth, or the ability to build lightweight internal apps without a separate low-code platform matter more than a slick modern interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is XWiki
paragraphs: XWiki is an open-source enterprise wiki platform that has been under continuous development since 2004. It is built and maintained by XWiki SAS, a company based in Paris, France, and is distributed under the LGPL open-source license, meaning the core software can be downloaded, self-hosted, and used commercially without paying a license fee.

What sets XWiki apart from simpler wiki tools is its dual identity as both a documentation platform and an application framework. Pages can contain structured data, forms, and scripted macros, which means teams can build small internal applications, such as inventories, issue trackers, or approval workflows, directly inside the wiki rather than relying on a separate tool.

XWiki is commonly used by IT departments, universities, government agencies, and software teams that want an enterprise-grade knowledge base they fully control, either self-hosted on their own infrastructure or run on XWiki's managed cloud offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: XWiki supports both WYSIWYG editing and wiki syntax, along with OpenDocument-based import and export, page annotations, tagging, and threaded comments, making it usable by both technical and non-technical staff.
Permissions are granular, letting administrators control access at the wiki, space, or individual page level, and every edit is versioned so changes can be reviewed or rolled back at any time.

The platform supports wiki farms, meaning a single installation can host many separate wikis for different teams or departments, and it exposes a RESTful API and extension marketplace so it can be integrated with external systems and extended with community-built applications.
Higher-tier commercial plans add single sign-on through OIDC or SAML, dedicated migration tooling from Confluence and SharePoint, and priority support with a named account manager.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The XWiki Community edition is entirely free and can be self-hosted with no cap on the number of users, which is the option most technical teams start with.

For organizations that want support, security patches, or managed cloud hosting, XWiki SAS sells four annual subscription tiers: Starter for up to 25 users on the cloud, Basic for up to 1,000 users on cloud or on-premise, Business for up to 10,000 users with SSO and migration tools, and Enterprise for unlimited users with advanced deployment support.

Professional services such as consulting and training are billed separately on a per-day basis, and open-source projects or NGOs can qualify for discounted support pricing and free entry-level cloud hosting.

features.title: WYSIWYG and wiki-syntax editing
features.description: Edit content visually or with lightweight wiki markup, with OpenDocument-based import and export for interoperability with office suites.

features.title: Built-in application framework
features.description: Turn wiki pages into structured data and scripted mini-applications without needing a separate low-code platform.

features.title: Granular rights management
features.description: Control read, edit, and admin permissions at the wiki, space, or page level for fine-grained access control.

features.title: Full version history and annotations
features.description: Every page change is tracked and reversible, and users can leave inline annotations and comments for collaborative review.

features.title: Wiki farms
features.description: Run multiple independent wikis for different teams or departments from a single installation.

features.title: Extension marketplace
features.description: Add community-built applications, macros, and integrations through XWiki's extension repository.

features.title: RESTful API
features.description: Integrate XWiki content and structured data with external systems and custom tooling.

features.title: Enterprise SSO on paid tiers
features.description: Business and Enterprise plans add OIDC and SAML single sign-on plus migration tooling from Confluence and SharePoint.

pros: Free, fully open-source core with no per-user pricing or seat caps
Doubles as a lightweight application-building platform, not just a documentation wiki
Strong version history and granular permissions suited to enterprise knowledge bases
Can be fully self-hosted for organizations with data residency or control requirements
Over two decades of continuous open-source development with an active extension ecosystem
cons: The interface feels dated next to modern SaaS knowledge tools like Notion or Confluence
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance and technical know-how
Confluence and SharePoint migration tools are reserved for Business-tier and above
Building custom applications on top of wiki pages has a learning curve for non-developers

faqs.question: Is XWiki free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community edition is free and open-source and can be self-hosted with no limit on users. Paid plans add support, security patches, and cloud hosting.

faqs.question: Is XWiki open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, XWiki is distributed under the LGPL license and its source code is publicly available.

faqs.question: Can XWiki replace Confluence?
faqs.answer: Many organizations use XWiki as a self-hosted Confluence alternative, and paid tiers include dedicated tooling to help migrate content from Confluence.

faqs.question: Does XWiki offer managed cloud hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, XWiki SAS offers cloud-hosted plans starting with the Starter tier, in addition to self-hosted deployment.

faqs.question: Can XWiki be used to build applications, not just wiki pages?
faqs.answer: Yes, XWiki includes a scripting and macro layer that lets users create structured data forms and small internal applications inside the wiki.

faqs.question: Does XWiki support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: OIDC and SAML single sign-on are available starting at the Business tier and above.

faqs.question: How is XWiki different from MediaWiki?
faqs.answer: XWiki adds structured data, application-building macros, and enterprise permissions on top of standard wiki editing, whereas MediaWiki is closer to a pure documentation wiki.

faqs.question: Who is behind XWiki?
faqs.answer: XWiki is developed by XWiki SAS, a company founded in 2004 and headquartered in Paris, France, with the software released under an open-source license.

enrichment.short_description: Local-first desktop API client for testing REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and SSE requests, built as a privacy-focused alternative to Postman and Insomnia.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for personal use; commercial license from $79 per year per individual
enrichment.seo_title: Yaak Review: Local-First API Client, Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Yaak, a local-first desktop API client for REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and SSE testing, plus its features, open-source license, and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Yaak is a desktop API client used to build, send, and debug HTTP, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events requests. It was launched in January 2024 by Mountain Loop Labs, a company founded by the same developer who previously created and sold the popular API client Insomnia. Yaak was built as a response to what its creator described as existing API tools changing for the worse after acquisition, positioning it as a developer-first, privacy-respecting alternative to Postman and Insomnia. The defining characteristic of Yaak is its local-first architecture: requests, collections, and configuration are stored as plain text files on the user's machine rather than in a cloud account, and the application works fully offline without requiring sign-up or an internet connection. This design also makes Yaak naturally compatible with Git, since collections can be versioned and shared through a normal Git repository rather than a proprietary sync service. Sensitive values such as API keys and tokens can be stored using ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption for local secrets management. Beyond core request building, Yaak supports request chaining and dynamic templating with variables, multiple windows for working on several projects simultaneously, over 30 customizable UI themes, and a CLI plus agent-oriented tooling for scripting and automation. It also offers direct import from Postman, Insomnia, OpenAPI specifications, curl commands, and Swagger files, easing migration for teams switching tools. Yaak's source code is MIT-licensed and publicly available on GitHub under the mountain-loop organization, so developers can self-build and use it entirely for free, including for commercial purposes, by compiling from source. The official pre-built desktop application is free for personal, non-commercial use with a 30-day full-featured trial and no credit card required. Using it at work requires a paid license: an Individual license costs $79 per year or $349 as a one-time lifetime purchase, covering commercial use on up to three devices with email support; a Business license costs $149 per user per year and adds transferable seats, SSO and user provisioning, and priority support for teams. Both paid tiers include a fallback license that keeps the currently installed version usable even after a subscription lapses. Yaak is best suited to individual developers and small to mid-sized engineering teams who want a fast, native, offline-capable API client without mandatory cloud accounts or telemetry.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Yaak
paragraphs: Yaak is a desktop API client for building and testing HTTP, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events requests. It was created by Mountain Loop Labs, founded by the developer behind Insomnia, and launched in January 2024 as a local-first alternative to cloud-account-based API tools.

The core idea behind Yaak is that developer tools should not require an account, an internet connection, or trust in a third-party cloud to function. Requests and collections are stored as plain text files on the local machine, which makes them naturally version-controllable with Git and portable between machines.

Yaak targets individual developers and small engineering teams who want a fast, native API client with strong privacy defaults, while still offering a paid commercial license and team features for organizations that need them.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Yaak supports multiple protocols in a single client, including REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events, so developers do not need separate tools for each type of API.
Its local-first architecture stores collections as plain text and integrates with Git for version control, while secrets and credentials can be stored using ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption.

Additional productivity features include request chaining and dynamic templating with variables, multiple windows for parallel projects, a CLI and agent support for automation, and over 30 customizable interface themes.
Migration is supported through direct import from Postman, Insomnia, OpenAPI, curl, and Swagger, making it straightforward to move existing collections into Yaak.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Yaak's source code is MIT-licensed, so developers can build it from source and use it for free, including commercially, without purchasing a license.
The official pre-built desktop application is free for personal, non-commercial use and includes a 30-day full-featured trial with no credit card required.

Commercial use requires a paid license: Individual is $79 per year or $349 as a one-time lifetime purchase for a single person on up to three devices, while Business is $149 per user per year and adds transferable licenses, SSO, user provisioning, and priority support for teams. Non-profits and bulk purchasers can contact Yaak's sales team for discounts.

features.title: Multi-protocol support
features.description: Send REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events requests from one client.

features.title: Local-first storage
features.description: Collections and requests are saved as plain text files on your machine, with no mandatory cloud account.

features.title: Git-friendly collections
features.description: Version and share API collections through a standard Git repository instead of a proprietary sync service.

features.title: Encrypted secrets
features.description: Store API keys and tokens locally using ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption.

features.title: Import from other tools
features.description: Bring in existing collections directly from Postman, Insomnia, OpenAPI, curl, and Swagger.

features.title: Request chaining and templating
features.description: Chain requests together and reuse dynamic variables across a collection.

features.title: CLI and automation support
features.description: Run and script requests outside the GUI for CI or agent-driven workflows.

features.title: Customizable themes
features.description: Choose from more than 30 interface themes to personalize the workspace.

pros: Free for personal use with no forced account creation or cloud sync
Fully offline-capable, local-first architecture appeals to privacy-conscious developers
MIT-licensed source code allows free commercial use for those who self-build
Git-native collection storage simplifies team version control
Straightforward import from Postman, Insomnia, and OpenAPI eases migration
cons: Commercial use of the pre-built app requires a paid license even for solo freelancers
Newer product (launched 2024) with a smaller ecosystem than Postman
Some team collaboration features are limited to the paid Business tier
Detailed public information on company size and roadmap is limited

faqs.question: Is Yaak free?
faqs.answer: Yaak is free for personal, non-commercial use. A paid Individual or Business license is required for use at work.

faqs.question: Is Yaak open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Yaak's source code is MIT-licensed and available on GitHub, allowing free use by anyone who builds it from source.

faqs.question: What protocols does Yaak support?
faqs.answer: Yaak supports HTTP/REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket, and Server-Sent Events requests.

faqs.question: Can I import my Postman or Insomnia collections into Yaak?
faqs.answer: Yes, Yaak supports direct import from Postman, Insomnia, OpenAPI, curl, and Swagger.

faqs.question: Who created Yaak?
faqs.answer: Yaak was built by Mountain Loop Labs, founded by the developer who previously created and sold Insomnia.

faqs.question: Does Yaak store my data in the cloud?
faqs.answer: No, Yaak is local-first and stores requests and collections as plain text files on your machine rather than in a mandatory cloud account.

faqs.question: How much does the Business license cost?
faqs.answer: The Business license is $149 per user per year and includes SSO, user provisioning, and priority support.

faqs.question: Does Yaak work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, Yaak is designed to work fully offline without requiring an internet connection.

use_cases.title: Team API collection sharing

use_cases.title: Offline or privacy-sensitive testing
use_cases.description: Test internal or sensitive APIs without sending collection data to a third-party cloud service.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source form and survey builder that lets communities and organizations create and self-host online forms without relying on Google Forms.
enrichment.seo_title: Yakforms Review: Free Open-Source Form Builder Alternative to Google Forms
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn about Yakforms, a free, self-hostable open-source form and survey builder that grew out of Framasoft's Framaforms project, plus its features and license.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Yakforms is a free, open-source form and survey builder that lets individuals and organizations create, publish, and collect responses from online forms without depending on a proprietary service like Google Forms or SurveyMonkey. The software originated as Framaforms, a tool officially released on October 5, 2016 by the French non-profit Framasoft as part of its De-google-ify Internet campaign, an initiative aimed at providing free, privacy-respecting alternatives to mainstream tech services. After maintaining Framaforms for roughly five years, Framasoft chose to hand the software back to its community around 2020 to 2021, renaming it Yakforms to avoid confusion between the software itself and the framaforms.org hosted service, which Framasoft continued to operate separately. Yakforms is built on Drupal 7 and makes extensive use of the Webform and Form Builder Drupal modules, and it is distributed under the GPL 2.0 license, meaning anyone can inspect, modify, self-host, and redistribute it. Because there is no central company behind Yakforms, development, translation, documentation, and interface design are driven by community contributors, and the project explicitly states that participation does not require technical skill. Functionally, Yakforms lets users build forms with a range of field types including text fields, text areas, checkboxes, and radio buttons, publish forms via a shareable web link or embed them on a website, collect and analyze submissions with basic built-in analytics and response visualizations, and export collected data as CSV or spreadsheet files for further processing. Organizations that want full control over their data can install their own private Yakforms instance rather than relying on a shared public instance, which supports the project's broader goal of contributing to a more decentralized internet. Notable adopters have included the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Framasoft's own hosted instances, and the ecosystem reportedly includes several active community-run instances used by thousands of form creators. Yakforms has no listed pricing because it is entirely free, community-supported software; any cost comes from self-hosting infrastructure or optional support arrangements an organization might set up independently. It is best suited to nonprofits, public institutions, and privacy-conscious organizations in French- and English-speaking communities who want a Google Forms alternative they can fully control and host themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Yakforms
paragraphs: Yakforms is a free, open-source form and survey builder that grew out of Framaforms, a tool released in 2016 by the French non-profit Framasoft as part of its campaign to offer alternatives to mainstream tech services like Google Forms.

After maintaining Framaforms for about five years, Framasoft returned stewardship of the software to its community, renaming it Yakforms to separate the open-source project from the framaforms.org hosted service it continued to run.
Built on Drupal 7 and licensed under GPL 2.0, Yakforms can be self-hosted by any organization that wants full ownership of its form and survey data instead of relying on a third-party SaaS provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can build forms with common field types such as text fields, text areas, checkboxes, and radio buttons, then publish them through a shareable web link or by embedding the form directly on a website.
Collected responses can be reviewed through built-in analytics with basic graphs and visualizations, and exported as CSV or spreadsheet files for deeper analysis elsewhere.

Because Yakforms is self-hostable, organizations can install their own private instance to keep full control of respondent data, which has made it popular with public institutions and privacy-focused nonprofits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Yakforms is entirely free software with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees, since it is released under the GPL 2.0 open-source license.
Any cost associated with using Yakforms comes from self-hosting infrastructure, such as server and maintenance costs, rather than from the software itself.
Community-run public instances are also available for organizations that prefer not to self-host, though data control is stronger when running a private installation.

features.title: Drag-and-drop form fields
features.description: Build forms using text fields, text areas, checkboxes, and radio buttons without writing code.

features.title: Shareable and embeddable forms
features.description: Publish forms via a direct link or embed them on an existing website.

features.title: Response analytics
features.description: Review submissions with basic charts and graphs directly inside the tool.

features.title: CSV and spreadsheet export
features.description: Download collected responses for further processing in external tools.

features.title: Self-hosting support
features.description: Install a private instance on your own server to keep full control of form data.

features.title: GPL 2.0 open-source license
features.description: Freely inspect, modify, and redistribute the software under an open-source license.

features.title: Community-driven development
features.description: Development, translation, and documentation are maintained by volunteer contributors.

pros: Completely free with no subscription or usage fees
Self-hostable, giving organizations full control over respondent data
Simple, no-code form-building interface suited to non-technical users
Open-source and community-maintained, reducing vendor lock-in risk
Proven track record through public institution and nonprofit adoption
cons: Built on Drupal 7, an aging technology stack compared to modern form builders
No dedicated company behind it, so support depends on community responsiveness
Interface and feature set are more basic than commercial tools like Typeform
Self-hosting requires server administration knowledge

faqs.question: Is Yakforms free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Yakforms is completely free and open-source under the GPL 2.0 license.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between Yakforms and Framaforms?
faqs.answer: Yakforms is the renamed continuation of the Framaforms software, originally released by Framasoft in 2016 and handed back to the community around 2020 to 2021.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Yakforms?
faqs.answer: Yes, organizations can install their own private Yakforms instance on their own server for full control over data.

faqs.question: What technology is Yakforms built on?
faqs.answer: Yakforms is built on Drupal 7, using the Webform and Form Builder Drupal modules.

faqs.question: Can I export form responses?
faqs.answer: Yes, responses can be exported as CSV or spreadsheet files.

faqs.question: Who maintains Yakforms?
faqs.answer: Yakforms is maintained by a volunteer community rather than a single company, after Framasoft handed the project over.

faqs.question: Is Yakforms available in English?
faqs.answer: Yes, while the project originated in the French-speaking Framasoft community, Yakforms is available in English as well.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, open-source social micro-blogging ecosystem built on the twtxt format, where users store and control their own posts as plain text files.
enrichment.seo_title: yarn.social Review: Decentralized twtxt Social Network, Features and Cost
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn about yarn.social, a small open-source decentralized micro-blogging ecosystem built on twtxt, including its clients, servers, and free cost model.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: yarn.social is a decentralized, open-source ecosystem for social micro-blogging built around the twtxt text-file format, created around July 2020 by James Mills, a former Facebook engineer who also goes by the online handle prologic. The project began when Mills discovered twtxt, a simple specification in which a person's posts, or twts, are stored as lines in a plain text file, typically named twtxt.txt, hosted anywhere on the web. Mills built a client for it, then extended the concept into a fuller federated implementation with threaded conversations, mentions, and user discovery, initially launching as twtxt.net before rebranding to yarn.social to reduce confusion with the underlying twtxt specification. Unlike mainstream social networks, yarn.social has no central company, administrator, or algorithm controlling the experience. Every component in the ecosystem, including clients, servers known as pods, and supporting tools, is open source and released under the AGPLv3 license, so anyone can run their own pod, write their own client, or simply host a personal twtxt.txt file on any web server they control. The ecosystem includes multiple interchangeable clients, such as web applications, terminal-based clients, and desktop apps, along with a gateway that bridges yarn.social pods with the wider ActivityPub Fediverse, plus tools for search, discovery, and RSS or Atom feed bridging so users can follow traditional blogs alongside social posts. There is no cost to use yarn.social: there are no ads, no tracking, and no paywalls, consistent with its goal of being a genuinely user-owned alternative to corporate social platforms. Because yarn.social is a community-run, volunteer-maintained project rather than a company, there is no fixed headquarters, employee count, or revenue figure to report, and public information about its total user base is limited; it should be understood as a niche, technically-oriented project popular mainly among developers and decentralization enthusiasts rather than a mainstream social network. Its core appeal is philosophical as much as functional: posts belong to the user as literal files they control, discovery and following work across independently hosted pods rather than one company's servers, and the entire stack can be self-audited since it is fully open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is yarn.social
paragraphs: yarn.social is a decentralized, open-source social micro-blogging ecosystem built on top of twtxt, a simple specification where a person's posts are stored as lines in a plain text file hosted on any web server they control.

The project was created around July 2020 by James Mills, a former Facebook engineer who built an extended, federated implementation of twtxt with threaded conversations, mentions, and user discovery, originally as twtxt.net before rebranding to yarn.social.

There is no central company behind yarn.social. It is maintained as a community, open-source project with no algorithm, no central server, and no single point of control, making it a niche alternative to mainstream corporate social networks rather than a mass-market product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Posts are stored as plain text twtxt.txt files that users host themselves, meaning content ownership sits with the individual rather than a platform operator.
The ecosystem offers multiple interchangeable clients, including web apps, terminal clients, and desktop applications, along with independently run servers called pods that anyone can host.

An ActivityPub gateway connects yarn.social to the wider Fediverse, and additional tools support search, discovery, and bridging RSS or Atom feeds so users can follow traditional blogs alongside social posts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: yarn.social is entirely free to use, with no ads, no tracking, and no paywalls anywhere in the ecosystem.

Because every component is open source under the AGPLv3 license, individuals and organizations can self-host their own pod or client at no licensing cost, with the only expense being their own server infrastructure.
There are no commercial pricing tiers, since the project is community-maintained rather than operated by a company selling a product.

features.title: Plain-text post ownership
features.description: Posts live as lines in a twtxt.txt file the user hosts and controls, rather than in a corporate database.

features.title: Federated pods
features.description: Independent servers, called pods, can be self-hosted and interoperate with each other rather than relying on one central service.

features.title: Multiple clients
features.description: Choose from web, terminal, and desktop clients that all work with the same open twtxt-based feeds.

features.title: ActivityPub gateway
features.description: Bridges yarn.social with the broader Fediverse, including platforms like Mastodon.

features.title: Threaded conversations and mentions
features.description: Adds structured replies and mentions on top of the simple twtxt format.

features.title: RSS and Atom bridging
features.description: Follow traditional blogs alongside social posts within the same feed reader experience.

features.title: Fully open specifications
features.description: Open specs mean anyone can build a compatible client or server without permission from a gatekeeper.

pros: Completely free with no ads, tracking, or paywalls
Users genuinely own their content as plain text files they control
Fully decentralized with no single company or server that can shut the network down
Open specifications allow anyone to build compatible clients or servers
Bridges to the wider Fediverse through ActivityPub
cons: Very small, niche user base compared to mainstream social platforms
No official company or support channel, since it is entirely community-run
Setup and self-hosting require more technical comfort than a typical social app
Public data on total users, activity levels, and growth is limited

faqs.question: Is yarn.social free?
faqs.answer: Yes, yarn.social is completely free with no ads, tracking, or paywalls.

faqs.question: Who created yarn.social?
faqs.answer: It was created around 2020 by James Mills, also known by the handle prologic, building on the earlier twtxt specification.

faqs.question: Is yarn.social open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, every component, including clients and server software known as pods, is open source under the AGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Does yarn.social connect to Mastodon or the Fediverse?
faqs.answer: Yes, an ActivityPub gateway bridges yarn.social pods with the wider Fediverse, including Mastodon.

faqs.question: What is twtxt?
faqs.answer: twtxt is the simple text-file specification that yarn.social is built on, where a user's posts are stored as lines in a plain text file they host.

faqs.question: Can I self-host my own yarn.social pod?
faqs.answer: Yes, anyone can run their own pod since the server software is open source and self-hostable.

faqs.question: How large is the yarn.social community?
faqs.answer: It is a small, niche community focused on developers and decentralization enthusiasts; precise user numbers are not publicly documented.

enrichment.short_description: AI avatar and video generation platform for creating talking-avatar videos, real-time video agents, and multilingual dubbing at scale.
enrichment.starting_price: From $20 per month (Basic plan), billed monthly or annually
enrichment.seo_title: Yepic AI Review: AI Avatar Video Generator Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Yepic AI's AI avatar and video agent platform works, including pricing tiers, key features, and use cases for multilingual video creation.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Yepic AI is an AI-powered video generation platform headquartered in London, United Kingdom, founded in 2019 by Aaron Jones and Jakub Langr. The company builds tools for creating synthetic video content featuring AI avatars, positioning itself as a video generator app used by more than 20,000 companies globally. Its core offering lets businesses generate videos of customizable digital avatars speaking scripted or dynamic content in more than 120 languages and dialects, using text-to-speech and lip-sync technology, including a proprietary dubbing engine called VidVoice for localizing existing video content into other languages. Beyond pre-recorded avatar videos, Yepic AI offers Video Agents, which are real-time, conversational AI avatars powered by large language models such as GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and GPT-4o, enabling interactive use cases like AI-driven customer support, sales conversations, and training simulations rather than static video output. The platform also includes facial recognition and emotion-detection technology designed to let avatars respond to a viewer's expressions during live interactions. Yepic AI's product suite is organized around Studio Express for quick, simple avatar video creation, Studio Pro for more advanced professional production, an API for developers who want to embed avatar generation into their own applications, and the Video Agents and VidVoice products for real-time and dubbing use cases respectively. Pricing is structured as a credit-based subscription with several tiers. The Basic plan starts at $20 per month or $200 per year and includes 200 monthly credits, up to 10 custom talking photos, and 3 real-time video agents with Express-only access. The Creator plan is $79 per month or $790 per year with 1,000 monthly credits, unlimited custom talking photos, 5 video agents with integrations, and both Pro and Express access plus API availability. Creator Plus, at $199 per month or $1,999 per year, is the most popular tier and adds 2,000 monthly credits, unlimited custom avatars and agents, and a computer vision add-on. Enterprise-oriented tiers include AI Employee at $499 per month, AI Team Member at $1,999 per month for team-based concurrent usage, and a fully custom Enterprise plan for large-scale organizations. All paid plans remove video watermarks and offer faster rendering. Yepic AI is best suited to marketing teams, training and enablement teams, and customer-facing organizations that want to scale personalized or multilingual video content without traditional video production costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Yepic AI
paragraphs: Yepic AI is a London-based AI video generation platform founded in 2019 by Aaron Jones and Jakub Langr. It lets businesses create videos of customizable AI avatars speaking scripted content, and says it is used by more than 20,000 companies worldwide.

The platform generates avatar-led videos in more than 120 languages and dialects using text-to-speech and lip-sync technology, and also offers a dedicated multilingual dubbing engine called VidVoice for localizing existing footage.

Beyond static avatar videos, Yepic AI offers real-time conversational Video Agents built on large language models, extending the platform from one-way video production into interactive, AI-driven video conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Studio Express and Studio Pro provide different levels of complexity for creating avatar videos, from quick simple clips to more advanced professional production workflows.

Video Agents use large language models such as GPT-4o to power real-time, conversational AI avatars that can respond dynamically rather than following a fixed script, supported by facial recognition and emotion-detection technology.

An API is available for developers who want to integrate avatar and video generation directly into their own applications or products, and VidVoice provides multilingual dubbing for existing video content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Yepic AI uses a credit-based subscription model with tiers ranging from Basic at $20 per month up through Creator and Creator Plus at $79 and $199 per month respectively, each unlocking more monthly credits, avatars, and video agents.

Enterprise-oriented plans, AI Employee and AI Team Member, are priced at $499 and $1,999 per month and add unlimited feature access with human onboarding, dedicated support channels, and multiple concurrent sessions for teams.
A fully custom Enterprise plan is available for large organizations with specific scaling or integration needs, and all paid tiers remove watermarks and offer faster video rendering than lower tiers.

faqs.answer: Plans start at $20 per month for the Basic tier and scale up to custom Enterprise pricing, based on monthly credits and features.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source SIP session border controller, Class 4 softswitch, and real-time VoIP billing system for telecom carriers and wholesale voice providers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source); optional community support via Patreon
enrichment.seo_title: Yeti-Switch Review: Open-Source SIP Softswitch and VoIP Billing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Yeti-Switch, an open-source SIP session border controller, Class 4 softswitch, and real-time billing system built for VoIP carriers and wholesalers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Yeti-Switch is an open-source SIP Session Border Controller, Class 4 softswitch, and real-time VoIP billing system built for telecom carriers and wholesale voice providers who need to interconnect, route, and bill voice traffic at scale. It is built on top of SEMS, a high-performance SIP/RTP application server, and its GitHub organization describes the goal as VoIP infrastructure software for humans, aimed at simplifying the traditionally complex world of carrier-grade telephony switching. The platform combines several functions that carriers typically need separate products for: carrier interconnection and session border control, dynamic call routing algorithms supporting least-cost routing, load balancing, and capacity control, integrated real-time billing for both wholesale and retail VoIP services, a number portability database, and STIR/SHAKEN call authentication support for combating caller ID spoofing and robocalls. It also offers Microsoft Teams Direct Routing compatibility, letting carriers connect their infrastructure to Microsoft Teams telephony, along with a REST API and a web-based customer portal for managing accounts and configuration. Yeti-Switch is maintained as a community open-source project rather than by a named commercial company; its code is hosted on GitHub under the yeti-switch organization, and the project accepts community support and donations through Patreon. Public documentation and an active community forum are available, along with a demo system for evaluating the platform before deployment. Because it is open source, Yeti-Switch can be deployed and used without licensing fees, though carriers typically need in-house or contracted SIP and telecom engineering expertise to deploy and operate it in production, given the technical complexity of Class 4 softswitch infrastructure. Public information about the project's founding date, the individuals or organization originally behind it, and its funding or team size is limited, consistent with it being a specialized, community-maintained open-source project serving a technical niche within the broader VoIP and telecom industry rather than a company with public-facing corporate details. It is best suited to VoIP carriers, wholesale voice providers, and telecom engineering teams who need carrier-grade routing and billing infrastructure they can self-host and fully control, rather than businesses looking for an end-user calling or contact center application.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Yeti-Switch
paragraphs: Yeti-Switch is an open-source SIP Session Border Controller, Class 4 softswitch, and real-time VoIP billing system built on the SEMS SIP/RTP application server. It is aimed at telecom carriers and wholesale voice providers who need to interconnect and route large volumes of voice traffic.

Rather than being a single-purpose tool, Yeti-Switch combines session border control, call routing, and billing in one platform, which is a common requirement for carriers running Class 4 switching infrastructure between other telecom networks.
The project is maintained by the open-source Yeti-Switch community and hosted on GitHub, with documentation, a demo environment, and a public forum available for evaluation and support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core carrier features include dynamic routing with least-cost routing, load, and capacity control, along with integrated real-time billing for both wholesale and retail VoIP services.

The platform includes a number portability database, STIR/SHAKEN call authentication for combating spoofed caller ID and robocalls, and Microsoft Teams Direct Routing compatibility for connecting to Teams telephony.
Administration is handled through a REST API and a web-based customer portal, giving carriers programmatic and self-service ways to manage accounts, routes, and billing configuration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Yeti-Switch is free, open-source software with no licensing cost, since its code is publicly available under the yeti-switch GitHub organization.
The project accepts voluntary community support and donations through Patreon rather than charging for the software itself.

Organizations deploying Yeti-Switch in production should budget for their own hosting infrastructure and SIP or telecom engineering expertise, since no official paid support tier is advertised on the project's site.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source CRM and ERP platform with over 80 modules covering sales, marketing, projects, support, finance, and HR for businesses and institutions.
enrichment.seo_title: YetiForce Review: Free Open-Source CRM and ERP Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what YetiForce offers: a free, open-source CRM and ERP platform with 80-plus modules for sales, support, finance, and HR, plus its features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: YetiForce is a free, open-source CRM and ERP platform originally developed in Poland, founded in 2014 by Blazej Pabiszczak and headquartered in Warsaw. The software was originally branched from Vtiger CRM, but after years of extensive modification and independent development it has grown into a distinct system with its own architecture and feature set. YetiForce is aimed at medium and large businesses as well as government institutions that need to manage multiple departments and business processes from a single platform, rather than being a narrowly focused sales-only CRM. The platform ships with more than 80 ready-made modules covering marketing, sales, project management, customer support, finance, logistics, human resources, and reporting, and includes a dynamic report builder for turning operational data into business decisions. Other capabilities include email integration, offline installation support, a responsive interface, and customizable workflows that let organizations adapt the system to their specific processes without needing a completely custom build. Because YetiForce is entirely free and open source, it places no restrictions on the number of users an organization can add, which the vendor highlights as a key differentiator from typical per-seat CRM pricing. According to the company, YetiForce is used by roughly 23,000 companies across more than 100 countries, and its largest documented single deployment involved more than 12,500 users, illustrating that it can scale to sizable enterprise and government deployments despite being free. The software supports more than 35 languages, reflecting its international user base beyond its Polish origins. YetiForce has received recognition from review platforms including SourceForge, G2, and Capterra, and was named the number one most affordable CRM software in a 2017 Capterra ranking of more than 500 CRM applications. The company behind the platform is registered as STOHID Technology S.A., based in Warsaw, Poland. Because YetiForce is free to deploy, its practical costs come primarily from self-hosting infrastructure, implementation, and any optional paid customization or support services an organization arranges independently, rather than from software licensing. It is best suited to organizations that want a highly customizable, no-license-fee CRM and ERP system and are comfortable managing their own hosting and technical setup, rather than those seeking a fully managed SaaS CRM.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is YetiForce
paragraphs: YetiForce is a free, open-source CRM and ERP platform founded in 2014 in Warsaw, Poland by Blazej Pabiszczak. It was originally branched from Vtiger CRM but has since diverged significantly through years of independent development.

Unlike a narrowly scoped sales CRM, YetiForce is designed as a broader business management platform, offering more than 80 modules that span marketing, sales, project management, support, finance, logistics, and HR.

The platform is used by an estimated 23,000 companies across more than 100 countries, according to the vendor, including large enterprises and government institutions, with its largest known deployment exceeding 12,500 users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: YetiForce includes ready-made modules across marketing, sales, project management, customer support, finance, logistics, HR, and reporting, giving organizations a single system for multiple departments.

A dynamic report builder lets users create custom reports from operational data, while customizable workflows allow the system to be adapted to specific business processes without a full custom build.
Additional capabilities include email integration, offline installation support, a responsive user interface, and support for more than 35 languages, reflecting its international deployment base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: YetiForce is completely free and open source, with no license fee and no restriction on the number of users an organization can add.
Costs come primarily from self-hosting infrastructure and implementation effort, since YetiForce is typically self-deployed rather than offered as a managed SaaS product.
Organizations that want additional customization or professional support can arrange this independently, as YetiForce does not publish tiered commercial pricing plans.

enrichment.short_description: Enterprise digital presence and location data platform that manages brand listings, reviews, local pages, and AI search visibility across the web.
enrichment.starting_price: From $199 per year per location (Emerging plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Yext Review: Enterprise Local SEO and AI Search Visibility Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Yext offers: an enterprise platform for managing location listings, reviews, local pages, and AI search visibility, plus its pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Yext is a publicly traded enterprise software company, listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker YEXT since 2017, founded in 2006 and headquartered in New York City. The company built its reputation as a location data and local SEO platform that helps multi-location brands keep their business information, such as addresses, hours, and phone numbers, accurate and consistent across search engines, maps, directories, and voice assistants. Over time Yext has expanded into what it now calls an agentic marketing platform, reflecting the shift in search behavior toward AI assistants and large language models alongside traditional search engines. Its core product is a Knowledge Graph that centralizes and verifies brand data as the single source of truth, which then feeds several connected modules: Listings Management syndicates and corrects business information across web directories and maps, Reviews handles reputation management and review monitoring across platforms, Pages generates search-optimized local landing pages for individual locations, and Content Distribution pushes verified content out to directories, maps, social platforms, and increasingly to large language models so AI assistants surface accurate brand information. A newer product called Scout provides real-time monitoring of how a brand appears in AI-powered search results and competitive intelligence around AI search visibility, reflecting Yext's positioning around the rise of AI-driven search and answer engines. Yext reports serving brands that collectively manage more than 4 million locations worldwide, with enterprise customers including Samsung, FedEx, Domino's, and IHG Hotels. Yext's published pricing follows a tiered, per-location annual subscription model: Emerging starts at $199 per year per location for basic listing distribution to around 30 directory sites; Essential is $449 per year and adds detailed listing management on premium sites like Google, Apple, Bing, Yahoo, and Yelp; Complete is $499 per year; and Premium is $999 per year for the most comprehensive feature set. Costs scale with the number of locations managed, and larger mid-market and enterprise customers typically negotiate custom volume-based contracts rather than paying the published per-location rates. Employee counts reported across data providers range from roughly 1,100 to over 2,500, reflecting differences in reporting dates and methodology. Yext is best suited to multi-location brands, franchises, healthcare systems, financial institutions, and large retailers that need centralized control over how their business information appears across an increasingly fragmented and AI-influenced search landscape.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Yext
paragraphs: Yext is a publicly traded enterprise software company, founded in 2006 and headquartered in New York City, listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker YEXT since 2017. It began as a location data and local SEO platform and has since expanded into what it calls an agentic marketing platform.

At its core, Yext helps multi-location brands keep business information such as addresses, hours, and phone numbers accurate and consistent across search engines, maps, directories, and voice assistants, using a centralized Knowledge Graph as the source of truth.

As search behavior shifts toward AI assistants and large language models, Yext has extended its platform to monitor and optimize how brands appear in AI-generated search results, in addition to traditional search and maps visibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Knowledge Graph centralizes verified brand data, which feeds Listings Management to syndicate accurate business information across directories and maps, and Reviews to monitor and manage reputation across review platforms.

Pages generates search-optimized local landing pages for individual business locations, while Content Distribution pushes verified content to directories, maps, social platforms, and large language models so AI assistants surface accurate brand information.

Scout is a newer capability focused on real-time monitoring of AI search visibility and competitive intelligence, reflecting Yext's positioning for the rise of AI-driven answer engines alongside traditional search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Yext publishes four fixed subscription tiers billed annually per location: Emerging at $199 per year, Essential at $449 per year, Complete at $499 per year, and Premium at $999 per year, with each tier unlocking distribution to more premium listing sites and additional features.
The Emerging plan covers basic listing distribution to roughly 30 directory sites, while Essential and above add management on premium sites such as Google, Apple, Bing, Yahoo, and Yelp.

Because pricing is per location, total cost scales with the number of business locations managed, and mid-market and enterprise buyers typically negotiate custom volume-based pricing rather than paying published rates.
